blacklist.note: These bans target corporate or AI-flavored use, not literal meaning. "Heat transforms ice into water" is allowed. "Transform your workflow" is banned. "The compiler optimizes the code" is allowed. "Optimize your potential" is banned. The test: does the word point at something specific in the sentence, or is it decoration? Decoration is banned. Words with no literal use (delve, leverage, tapestry of insights, embark on a journey, navigate the frontier) have no exception, they are banned in every form.

tier_1_always_banned.summary: AI fingerprints and corporate or decorative patterns with no literal use. Banned in every form, no exception. If a draft contains any of these, the sentence is rewritten from scratch.

section.name: Sentence templates and structural shells
section.summary: The skeletons of AI writing. Even clean vocabulary cannot hide these. These are the MOST detectable AI patterns. Rewrite from scratch.

group.name: Paired constructions (AI-flavored variants)
group.item: It is not just about X, it is also about Y
It's not just X, Y also matters
Not only does it X, but it also Y

group.name: Named structural patterns
group.pattern: The "not X, it's Y" move. Latin: correctio. Greek: epanorthosis. The speaker states something, then pretends to retract it so the second clause lands harder. BANNED ALTOGETHER in every form: one sentence ("It's not X, it's Y."), two sentences ("It is not X. It is Y."), softened ("It's not just X, it's Y." / "Not so much X as Y."), and dressed-up variants like definition-then-reframe ("X is defined as Y. But what it really means is Z."). If Y is the real claim, write only Y. The reader does not need X denied first.
"The question is not whether X, the question is Y." A correctio built around repeating the same noun. Just state Y.
"The result: [dramatic claim]." Write a normal sentence.
"But why does this matter? Because..." Either let the question sit or make the statement directly.
"It is not just A. It is B. It is C." Ascending drama is inflation, not emphasis. This is correctio stacked three times.
"Imagine X. Now imagine Y. Now imagine Z." One is an invitation. A chain is manipulation.
"Remember the story from the beginning? It turns out..." If the connection is strong, the reader already made it.

group.name: Framing shells
group.item: By doing X, you can achieve Y
The [noun] has revolutionized the way we [verb]
The purpose of this [article/guide] is to...
This [tool/approach] is a game changer for...
As we navigate the complexities of...

section.name: Punctuation and formatting tells
section.summary: Formatting choices that instantly reveal AI origin. These are the second most detectable patterns.

group.name: Em dashes
group.rule: Never use. Rewrite as two sentences or use a comma, period, or colon.

group.name: Inflated assertions
group.rule: If the writing did its job, the reader already believes. If it did not, an assertion will not fix it. Cut these.

group.name: Dramatic fragmentation
group.rule: Fragmenting sentences for emphasis is performative. Delete.

group.name: Emotional imperatives
group.rule: Never tell the reader when to pause or what to feel.

group.name: Triple-adjective lists
group.item: "It is powerful, transformative, and deeply compelling."
group.rule: Pick one word. Three means the point is weak.

section.name: Buzzword verbs (no literal use)
section.summary: These add volume, not meaning. They have no specific meaning to recover. Replace with the specific thing you actually mean.

section.name: Inflators with no literal use
section.summary: These modify nothing. They make sentences feel important without adding information.

section.name: Openers and lead-ins
section.summary: Never open a sentence, paragraph, or section with any of these.

group.name: Time-based framing
group.item: In the rapidly shifting landscape of
In the constantly evolving field of

group.name: Importance markers
group.item: It is important to note that
In light of the fact that
In spite of the fact that

section.name: Robotic connectors and summary markers
section.summary: These make writing feel assembled from parts. Two good sentences rarely need a bridge word.

section.name: Filler and padding
section.summary: Delete these and check if the sentence survives. It almost always does.

section.name: Abstract nouns with no domain use
section.summary: These replace concrete detail with corporate air. They have no literal use that the sentence needs.

section.name: Metaphor and analogy tropes
section.summary: These substitute drama for substance. Unless a metaphor genuinely helps the reader understand something new, it is noise.

group.name: Action and adventure tropes (never use)
group.item: Paving the way for a brighter tomorrow

section.name: Outro and conclusion patterns
section.summary: Never close a section with these. They signal the writer ran out of things to say.

group.name: Cliched openers
group.item: At the end of the day

group.name: Reflective fillers
group.item: With all of this in mind

group.name: Inference templates
group.item: As a result the takeaway is

tier_2_literal_exception.summary: Allowed ONLY when used with literal, specific meaning the sentence actually needs. Banned when used as decoration, intensifier, or corporate flavor. Apply the test from the note: does the word point at something specific, or is it filler? If filler, cut. If specific, keep.

group.name: Verbs banned when metaphorical or corporate, allowed when literal

verb.example_allowed: The key enables the door to open.
verb.example_banned: The course enables you to unlock your potential.
This will enhance your design thinking.

verb.example_allowed: The chef optimized the recipe to use half the salt.
The new form streamlines the application from 12 fields to 4.
Enzymes catalyze the breakdown of food in the stomach.
The speaker amplifies the sound of the guitar.
Marie Curie pioneered the study of radioactivity.

verb.allowed_use: literal coordination of musicians or processes

verb.example_allowed: The runner maximizes her speed in the last 100 meters.

group.name: Inflating modifiers banned as decoration, allowed when the word is precise

adjective.example_allowed: The crucial test came when the bridge held the full weight of the truck.
The pivotal year was 1969, when the moon landing happened.
adjective.example_banned: A pivotal moment in our journey.

adjective.allowed_use: literally necessary for life or function
adjective.example_banned: It is vital that you understand this.

adjective.allowed_use: literal physical agility, or named methodology
adjective.example_allowed: The cat was agile on the narrow ledge.

adjective.example_banned: An efficient solution to the problem.

adjective.example_allowed: The trial was exemplary, used as a reference for later cases.
The pocket knife is versatile, it cuts, opens cans, and tightens screws.
adjective.example_banned: A versatile tool for modern teams.

adjective.allowed_use: rare, when something genuinely produces a new insight

group.name: Connectors banned as filler, allowed when carrying real causal or contrastive weight
group.rule: Limit: at most one of these per package. Two or more in the same draft means they are filler again.

group.name: Hedging banned as decoration, allowed when softening a non-canonical fact
group.rule: The copywriting guide allows "Research suggests..." for broad claims about behavior. The same logic permits these constructions when the underlying claim genuinely is not textbook-canonical and needs softening. Banned when used to dodge ownership of a sentence the writer means flatly.

group.name: Empty in corporate use, real in their domain

noun.allowed_use: mathematical, chemical, or named technical solution
noun.example_allowed: The solution to the equation is x=4.

noun.allowed_use: legal, software, or research framework with a specific name
noun.example_banned: A framework for thinking about creativity.

noun.allowed_use: biological ecosystem, or specific software ecosystem

noun.example_allowed: The methodology section of the paper.
She had the opportunity to study in Vienna.
The aircraft has the capability to refuel mid-air.
noun.example_banned: Core capabilities for the modern leader.

noun.allowed_use: academic Kuhnian sense, named scientific paradigm
noun.example_allowed: The Newtonian paradigm dominated physics until 1905.

group.name: Paired constructions
group.item: Not only X, but also Y
group.rule: The basic form is normal English and can stand. The AI-flavored variants live in tier 1, section 1.

group.name: Rare or made-up hyphenated words (use only when necessary)
group.rule: Not a hard ban. These sound clever but are harder to read. Default to "instant," "moment," or "a single beat." Keep the compound only when the plain word would lose meaning the sentence needs.
